title: Home Appliance Assembly Line Balancing
des_qualification_note: All required criteria satisfied: discrete appliance units flow through the line; discrete events (station start, station complete, transfer, defect detection, rework routing); explicit simulated clock across cycle times, takt times, and 10-hour shifts. Both typical criteria clearly present: 29-station air conditioner lines, 73-station refrigerator lines, and 30-50 operator crews create resource contention; service times range widely (33.36 to 152.11 seconds in the air conditioner data) with stochastic rework and defect rates.
summary_of_content_provenance: Rich but friction-heavy topic. One first-party fetch succeeded (PMC10949552 air conditioner line paper — First-party x Direct x Adjacent Method, because the paper uses decision tree ML rather than DES but its structural data is directly applicable). Two PDF extraction failures (NSF PAR line balancing case study; POMS refrigeration assembly conference paper) and one 403 (Visual Components Midea case study) would live in a deferred human_followup_queue. Two second-party direct DES sources captured from search snippets: Midea washing machine case (Visual Components simulation tool) and Egyptian refrigerator assembly case (multi-source aggregated). Multiple product families surface across sources (refrigerator, washing machine, air conditioner, dishwasher, dryer) and map to distinct model definition variants.
intended_audience_note: Targeted at early adopters from GE Appliances manufacturing engineering. Scenarios and examples are chosen for direct relevance to their production environment at Appliance Park in Louisville — refrigerator, washer, dryer, and dishwasher line work. The entry is vendor-neutral at the blueprint level but grounded in specific case studies from published manufacturing DES literature.
